fragment_text: Creatine monohydrate is described as the form with the strongest ergogenic support for increasing high-intensity exercise capacity and lean mass during training.
dose_context: not specified in this extracted fragment
conditions_or_limits: Indirect support for strength; it should be combined with strength-specific meta-analyses.
annotation_notes: Cleaned: project-specific interpretation was removed and support strength for M01 was downgraded.

fragment_text: After a loading phase, performance in high-intensity and/or repeated exercise typically increases, making creatine especially relevant for power claims or short repeated efforts.
dose_context: not specified in this extracted fragment
conditions_or_limits: More useful for high-intensity and repeated efforts than for purely aerobic events.
annotation_notes: Cleaned: text made more faithful to the position stand and focused on high intensity.

fragment_text: Creatine is also associated with increases in lean mass during training, although that support is complementary and does not justify broad muscle-gain promises on its own.
dose_context: not specified in this extracted fragment
conditions_or_limits: For body composition, it should also be supported by specific meta-analyses; the effect does not justify inflated claims.
annotation_notes: Kept as partial support for lean_mass_gain.

fragment_text: Compared with placebo, creatine combined with resistance training significantly increased upper- and lower-body strength in adults under 50 years of age.
dose_context: high-dose tended to outperform lower-dose protocols; exact threshold not fixed in snippet
conditions_or_limits: Benefits appear larger in men than in women, and the finding is limited to adults under 50 years of age.
annotation_notes: Primary fragment for backed results on explicit strength claims.

fragment_text: The combination of creatine and resistance training produced a small increase in direct measures of regional muscle hypertrophy.
dose_context: not specified in extracted snippet
conditions_or_limits: The effect size is small; it supports the claim only partially, not strong muscle-gain promises.
annotation_notes: Very useful for distinguishing partially_backed from potentially_misleading in aggressive muscle claims.

fragment_text: Caffeine improves performance when consumed at doses of 3-6 mg/kg, and the minimum effective dose may be lower; this provides a reasonable basis for claims about reduced perceived fatigue or increased alertness, but not for exaggerated energy promises.
dose_context: 3-6 mg/kg body mass; minimal effective dose may be ~2 mg/kg
conditions_or_limits: Very high doses do not appear to add benefit and increase the risk of adverse effects.
annotation_notes: Cleaned: downgraded to moderate because 'energy' is an indirect commercial translation of the ergogenic/RPE effect.

fragment_text: Caffeine has consistently shown exercise-performance benefits, especially at moderate doses, and is relevant for endurance/performance claims.
dose_context: 3-6 mg/kg body mass
conditions_or_limits: Individual response varies; tolerance and habitual intake can modulate the effect.
annotation_notes: No substantial changes; strong support for M05.

fragment_text: Acute caffeine intake can be used effectively as an ergogenic aid at moderate doses, producing a small but consistent improvement in endurance performance.
dose_context: moderate doses, typically 3-6 mg/kg
conditions_or_limits: The effect is small; it does not justify extreme claims.
annotation_notes: Cleaned: text made closer to the meta-analysis.

fragment_text: Caffeine reduces the subjective rating of effort during exercise, providing a specific basis for claims about reduced perceived fatigue.
dose_context: not standardized in this extracted fragment
conditions_or_limits: Perceptual outcome; it should not be extrapolated to a generic promise of extreme energy.
annotation_notes: Kept; it is a good specific complement for M04.

fragment_text: The review of whey for recovery of muscle function after resistance exercise found mixed results: only some studies reported benefits, and the literature is described as equivocal.
dose_context: not standardized in extracted snippet
conditions_or_limits: Whey-specific evidence for recovery is not uniform; it does not support strong accelerated-recovery claims.
annotation_notes: Major correction: the previous version overstated the support. It was revised to partial-weak support.

fragment_text: Peri-exercise protein may help preserve maximal strength and reduce creatine kinase after resistance exercise, but it showed no clear effect on muscle soreness.
dose_context: peri-exercise protein supplementation
conditions_or_limits: Indirect evidence for whey because the document addresses supplemental protein in general, not whey in isolation.
annotation_notes: Kept as partial and indirect support for M07.

fragment_text: Protein supplementation significantly improved changes in strength and muscle size during prolonged resistance training in healthy adults.
dose_context: benefit plateau beyond ~1.6 g/kg/day total protein intake
conditions_or_limits: Indirect evidence for whey: the meta-analysis is about supplemental protein in general, not specifically whey.
retrieval_keywords: protein supplementation|lean body mass|muscle size|resistance training
annotation_notes: Cleaned: downgraded from yes/strong to partial/moderate because whey specificity is lacking.

fragment_text: Above an approximate total daily intake of 1.6 g/kg/day, protein supplementation provided no additional benefits for fat-free mass or strength during resistance training.
dose_context: ~1.6 g/kg/day total protein intake threshold
conditions_or_limits: Limitation fragment; useful for constraining claims such as more protein always means more muscle.
annotation_notes: Kept as a very useful limitation fragment.

fragment_text: Beta-alanine supplementation showed a significant overall effect, with the largest effects appearing in exercises lasting 0.5 to 10 minutes, especially on exercise capacity rather than broad performance.
dose_context: not specified in extracted meta-analysis snippet
conditions_or_limits: The evidence is stronger for exercise capacity than for any generic performance claim.
annotation_notes: Cleaned: the time window and the capacity vs performance distinction are made explicit.

fragment_text: The ISSN position stand states that daily beta-alanine supplementation of 4 to 6 g for at least 2 to 4 weeks can improve exercise performance and act as an ergogenic aid.
dose_context: 4-6 g/day for at least 2-4 weeks
conditions_or_limits: More relevant to intense efforts; it is not universal support for every modality.
annotation_notes: Kept; a good fragment for rules involving dose and duration.

fragment_text: Caffeine significantly improves attention, alertness, and reaction time during and after exercise, with effects observed at doses as low as 1-3 mg/kg body mass.
dose_context: 1-6 mg/kg body mass
conditions_or_limits: Effects on complex cognitive tasks are less consistent than on simple reaction time and vigilance.
annotation_notes: Well-established cognitive ergogenic effect of caffeine from multiple meta-analyses.

fragment_text: Caffeine enhances vigilance and sustained attention during prolonged exercise or sleep deprivation, supporting claims about improved mental performance during physical activity.
dose_context: moderate doses typically 3-6 mg/kg
conditions_or_limits: Benefits are clearer for vigilance than for memory or complex decision-making.
annotation_notes: Complements FRAG_012_01 with the vigilance and sustained attention angle.

fragment_text: Citrulline supplementation increases plasma arginine concentrations and nitric oxide bioavailability, thereby enhancing blood flow and oxygen delivery to working muscles during exercise.
dose_context: 6-8 g citrulline malate or 3-6 g L-citrulline taken 40-60 min pre-exercise
conditions_or_limits: The magnitude of blood flow improvement varies across studies and populations.
annotation_notes: Based on Trexler et al. (2019) and Wax et al. (2015) findings.

fragment_text: Citrulline malate supplementation at 6-8 g may increase the number of repetitions to failure in resistance exercise by approximately 6-11%, suggesting improved exercise tolerance.
dose_context: 6-8 g citrulline malate pre-exercise
conditions_or_limits: Many studies have small sample sizes; the effect on aerobic time to exhaustion is less consistent.
retrieval_keywords: citrulline malate|repetitions|time to exhaustion|exercise tolerance|resistance exercise
annotation_notes: Moderate support; effect sizes vary and most studies are small.

fragment_text: Participants who consumed citrulline malate before exercise reported reduced perceived muscle soreness at 24 and 48 hours post-exercise compared to placebo, though the evidence base is limited.
dose_context: 8 g citrulline malate pre-exercise
conditions_or_limits: Based on a single well-cited study; replication is needed before strong claims are supported.
annotation_notes: Perez-Guisado and Jakeman (2010); limited evidence base.

fragment_text: Correcting vitamin D deficiency in athletes is associated with improved muscle function, including greater type II muscle fiber size and improved muscle strength, but supplementation above sufficient levels provides no additional performance benefit.
dose_context: 1000-5000 IU/day to achieve serum 25(OH)D above 40 ng/mL
conditions_or_limits: Benefits are primarily observed in individuals who are deficient; no ergogenic effect when vitamin D status is already sufficient.
retrieval_keywords: vitamin D|muscle strength|muscle function|deficiency|type II fiber
annotation_notes: Owens et al. (2018) and Close et al. (2013); benefits conditional on baseline status.

fragment_text: Vitamin D supplementation reduces the incidence of upper respiratory tract infections in athletes and the general population, with the greatest benefit seen in those with baseline deficiency.
dose_context: 1000-4000 IU/day for at least 12 weeks
conditions_or_limits: Greatest benefit when correcting deficiency; high-dose bolus supplementation is less effective than daily dosing.
annotation_notes: He et al. (2013) and Martineau et al. (2017); well-supported for immune health.

fragment_text: Omega-3 fatty acid supplementation at doses of 2-4 g EPA+DHA per day reduces circulating inflammatory markers including IL-6 and TNF-alpha after intense exercise, supporting an anti-inflammatory role in athletic recovery.
dose_context: 2-4 g combined EPA+DHA per day for at least 4 weeks
conditions_or_limits: Anti-inflammatory effects do not necessarily translate to improved performance; recovery benefits are indirect.
annotation_notes: Philpott et al. (2019); consistent anti-inflammatory evidence.

fragment_text: Fish oil supplementation may attenuate delayed onset muscle soreness after eccentric exercise, though the effect is small and not all studies show significant benefits.
dose_context: 2-3 g EPA+DHA per day
conditions_or_limits: Effects on DOMS are inconsistent across studies; the practical significance is uncertain.
annotation_notes: Mickleborough (2013); evidence is weak and inconsistent for DOMS specifically.

page_or_section: ISSN position stand summary (Wilson et al., 2013)
fragment_text: HMB supplementation at 3 g per day may help preserve lean body mass during caloric restriction or catabolic conditions, and has shown modest benefits for lean mass accretion in untrained individuals beginning resistance training.
dose_context: 3 g/day split into three 1 g doses
conditions_or_limits: Effects in well-trained athletes performing normal training are small to negligible; early dramatic findings have been questioned.
annotation_notes: ISSN Position Stand (Wilson et al. 2013); effects are population-dependent.

fragment_text: HMB supplementation reduces markers of exercise-induced muscle damage such as creatine kinase and lactate dehydrogenase, suggesting a protective role against muscle protein breakdown after intense exercise.
dose_context: 3 g/day for at least 2 weeks prior to damaging exercise
conditions_or_limits: The reduction in damage markers does not always correspond to faster functional recovery.
annotation_notes: Wilson et al. (2013) and Rowlands and Thomson (2009).

fragment_text: Sodium bicarbonate at 0.2-0.3 g/kg body mass is an effective ergogenic aid for high-intensity exercise lasting 30 seconds to 12 minutes, enhancing extracellular buffering capacity and delaying acidosis-related fatigue.
dose_context: 0.2-0.3 g/kg body mass taken 60-120 min pre-exercise
conditions_or_limits: Gastrointestinal distress is common and may offset performance benefits; individual response variability is high.
annotation_notes: ISSN Position Stand and Carr et al. (2011) meta-analysis; strong evidence for buffering effect.

fragment_text: Sodium bicarbonate supplementation consistently improves performance in repeated sprint protocols, swimming events, and combat sports where acid-base balance is a limiting factor.
dose_context: 0.2-0.3 g/kg body mass
conditions_or_limits: Benefits are specific to anaerobic/glycolytic events; not effective for purely aerobic or very short (<30s) activities.
annotation_notes: Carr et al. (2011) and Peart et al. (2012); consistent for repeated sprints.

fragment_text: Dietary nitrate supplementation from beetroot juice improves endurance exercise performance by 1-3% in sub-elite athletes, primarily by reducing the oxygen cost of submaximal exercise through enhanced mitochondrial efficiency.
dose_context: 6.4-12.8 mmol nitrate (approximately 500 mL beetroot juice) consumed 2-3 hours pre-exercise
conditions_or_limits: Effects are attenuated or absent in elite/highly trained athletes; acute dosing appears more effective than chronic supplementation.
annotation_notes: McMahon et al. (2017) and Jones (2014); effects strongest in sub-elite populations.

fragment_text: Beetroot juice supplementation extends time to exhaustion during moderate to high-intensity exercise, with the largest effects seen in exercises lasting 5-30 minutes where oxygen delivery is a limiting factor.
dose_context: 400-800 mg dietary nitrate pre-exercise
conditions_or_limits: Antibacterial mouthwash can blunt the nitrate-nitrite conversion pathway and reduce effectiveness.
annotation_notes: Jones et al. (2014); consistent for time-to-exhaustion protocols.

fragment_text: Ashwagandha root extract supplementation at 300-600 mg per day for 8-12 weeks modestly improves muscular strength and recovery in resistance-trained individuals, though effect sizes are small and study samples are limited.
dose_context: 300-600 mg/day standardized root extract for 8-12 weeks
conditions_or_limits: Most studies have small sample sizes; ashwagandha is not yet included in major ISSN or IOC position papers as a recommended ergogenic aid.
annotation_notes: Bonilla et al. (2021) and Wankhede et al. (2015); promising but limited evidence.

fragment_text: Ashwagandha supplementation significantly reduces perceived stress and serum cortisol levels by 14-28% compared to placebo, supporting claims about stress reduction and adaptation in physically active individuals.
dose_context: 300-600 mg/day for at least 8 weeks
conditions_or_limits: Long-term safety data are limited; extract standardization varies between products which complicates dosing recommendations.
annotation_notes: Chandrasekhar et al. (2012); consistent cortisol-reduction findings across RCTs.

fragment_text: Taurine supplementation at doses of 1-6 g improves endurance performance as measured by time-to-exhaustion, likely through its role as an antioxidant and support of calcium handling in skeletal muscle.
dose_context: 1-6 g acutely or 1-3 g/day for 1-2 weeks
conditions_or_limits: Limited evidence for peak power or strength outcomes; most studies are small and short-term.
annotation_notes: Waldron et al. (2018) Sports Medicine meta-analysis.

fragment_text: Taurine may reduce oxidative stress markers and support exercise tolerance, but evidence for direct perceived energy or fatigue reduction is limited and confounded by co-ingestion with caffeine in energy drink studies.
dose_context: 1-3 g per day
conditions_or_limits: Energy drink studies confound taurine effects with caffeine; isolated taurine evidence for subjective energy is weak.
annotation_notes: Zhang et al. (2004); limited isolated evidence apart from energy drink confounding.

fragment_text: Creatine supplementation may benefit cognitive processing and mental performance, particularly under conditions of sleep deprivation or mental fatigue, though evidence in well-rested healthy adults is less consistent.
dose_context: 5 g/day or standard loading protocol
conditions_or_limits: Cognitive benefits are most evident during stress conditions; the effect in well-rested non-fatigued individuals is unclear.
annotation_notes: Rae et al. (2003) and Avgerinos et al. (2018); benefits conditional on cognitive stress.

fragment_text: Whey protein consumption stimulates muscle protein synthesis in a dose-responsive manner, with approximately 20-40 g of high-quality protein per meal providing near-maximal stimulation of postprandial muscle protein synthesis rates.
dose_context: 20-40 g per meal; leucine content is a key driver
conditions_or_limits: The effect is on acute MPS rates; chronic hypertrophy outcomes depend on total daily protein intake and training stimulus.
annotation_notes: Jager et al. (2017) ISSN protein position stand; strong acute MPS evidence.
